article_title: This free Obsidian sync setup is better than Obsidian Premium
core_thesis: The article argues that using the free, open-source Obsidian Git plugin with a private GitHub repository provides a superior alternative to Obsidian Sync (the paid subscription service) for syncing Obsidian notes across devices. It claims this setup offers unlimited vaults, permanent version history, 15GB of free storage, and greater control and openness, while being just as convenient once configured.
detailed_summary: The article presents a step-by-step guide to replacing Obsidian Sync with a free alternative using the Obsidian Git plugin and GitHub. It begins by comparing the paid tiers: $5/month for one vault with 1GB storage and 30-day version history, or $10/month for up to 10 vaults with 12-month history. The free setup, in contrast, syncs unlimited vaults, provides permanent Git version history, and offers 15GB of free GitHub storage. The author emphasizes that this method gives users granular control over what gets synced, access to Git's diff and merge tools for conflict resolution, and freedom from Obsidian's closed platform—notes can be edited with any tool. The guide covers prerequisites (Obsidian AppImage on Linux, Git installed on all devices, a free GitHub account), creating a private GitHub repo, generating a personal access token, initializing Git in the vault folder, and installing/configuring the Obsidian Git plugin. The plugin can auto-sync at intervals or after typing pauses, and manual sync is available via the command palette. The article also includes a comment from an Obsidian employee noting that GitHub is not end-to-end encrypted, unlike Obsidian Sync, and that Microsoft could access notes. Other commenters suggest alternatives like Syncthing or Mega for privacy-conscious users.

entities.name: Obsidian Git
entities.role: Free, open-source Obsidian plugin that syncs notes via Git and GitHub

entities.name: GitHub
entities.role: Microsoft-owned platform providing free private repositories for storing the Obsidian vault

entities.name: Obsidian Sync
entities.role: Paid subscription service for syncing Obsidian notes, criticized for cost and limitations

entities.name: Faisal Rasool

entities.name: Steph (Obsidian employee)

key_data_points: Obsidian Sync costs $5/month for one vault with 1GB storage and 30-day version history, or $10/month for up to 10 vaults with 12-month history (🟢 relevant to your student budget: this free alternative saves money).

The Obsidian Git plugin uses a private GitHub repository to store the vault, offering unlimited vaults, permanent version history, and 15GB of free storage (🟢 relevant to your homelab/devops: leverages Git for version control, a skill you can reuse).

Setup requires Git installed on all devices, a free GitHub account, and about 15 minutes; the plugin can auto-sync at intervals or after typing pauses (🟢 relevant to your programming: reinforces Git workflow and automation).

An Obsidian employee commented that GitHub is not end-to-end encrypted, so Microsoft could access notes for AI training or other purposes, unlike Obsidian Sync which is E2EE (🟢 relevant to your cybersecurity/red teaming: highlights a privacy trade-off; consider encryption if handling sensitive data).

Commenters suggested alternatives like Syncthing (peer-to-peer, no cloud) or Mega (encrypted cloud storage) for those prioritizing privacy over Git's versioning features (🟢 relevant to your homelab: Syncthing is a self-hosted sync solution).
methodology_steps: Install the AppImage version of Obsidian on Linux (avoid Flatpak/Snap for compatibility).

Install Git on all devices: on Windows use `winget install --id Git.Git -e --source winget`, on macOS use `brew install git`, on Android install Termux and run `pkg install git` and `termux-setup-storage`.
Create a free GitHub account and a new private repository (select 'Private' visibility).
Generate a classic personal access token in GitHub Settings > Developer Settings > Personal Access Tokens > Tokens (classic). Enable the 'repo' scope and copy the token.
Navigate to your Obsidian vault folder in a file manager, right-click and open a terminal there.
Initialize Git with `git init`, then add the remote origin: `git remote add origin https://<token>@github.com/<username>/<repo>.git`.
In Obsidian, go to Community Plugins, search for 'obsidian git', install and enable it.
Configure the plugin: set auto-sync interval (e.g., 5 or 10 minutes) and enable auto-backup after typing pauses. Optionally enable auto-pull on startup.
To manually sync, press Ctrl+P, search for 'commit-and-sync' and execute it.
conclusions: The Obsidian Git plugin provides a free, feature-rich alternative to Obsidian Sync, with unlimited vaults, permanent version history, and 15GB storage, making it ideal for users who want cost savings and Git's powerful version control (🟢 relevant to your programming and homelab: reinforces Git skills and self-hosted solutions).

However, the setup lacks end-to-end encryption, meaning notes stored on GitHub are accessible to Microsoft, which may be a concern for sensitive data. Users who prioritize privacy should consider alternatives like Syncthing or encrypted cloud services (🟢 relevant to your cybersecurity: understand the trade-off between convenience and security).

The article's comment section reveals that while the free setup is technically superior in many ways, the official Obsidian Sync offers E2EE and supports the developers. The choice depends on whether you value cost/features over privacy and supporting open-source development.
personal_relevance_summary: This article directly addresses your need for cost-effective, self-hosted solutions (homelab/devops) by providing a free alternative to a paid subscription service. As a programmer, you'll benefit from the Git-based workflow, which reinforces version control skills applicable to your projects. For your cybersecurity/red teaming interests, the article highlights a critical privacy trade-off: using GitHub means your notes are not end-to-end encrypted, which is a security consideration if you store sensitive information. The step-by-step guide is practical and can be adapted to other self-hosted sync setups (e.g., using your own Git server). As a student, saving $5-10/month is valuable, and the permanent version history is great for tracking changes in study notes.
extra_advice: Consider enhancing this setup for better security: use a self-hosted Git server (e.g., Gitea or GitLab on your homelab) instead of GitHub to keep your notes fully private and under your control. Alternatively, encrypt your vault before pushing to GitHub using a tool like git-crypt or a GPG-based solution. This gives you the best of both worlds—free, unlimited versioning with end-to-end encryption.
